content:
TECHNOLOGICAL SOVEREIGNTY AND HUMAN CREATIVITY: REFLECTIONS FROM THE BRIDGE BETWEEN ART AND ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE

14 min read
Just now

--

I do not remember the exact date, but it was back in 1985 when a good friend from the Center for Advanced Studies of Blanes (CEAB) invited me to share my perspective on some research that he and his team were developing. They wanted to know my opinion as an artist. It was an era when interdisciplinary work was just beginning to gain ground, and the idea that a painter could contribute something to scientific research seemed as novel as it was stimulating.
Later, in 1994, this team relocated to the campus of the Autonomous University of Barcelona (UAB), and I received another invitation. The question hanging in the air was fascinating: What does a visual artist think of artificial intelligence?
I once heard this friend of mine say something that would resonate within me for decades: “New technologies, and artificial intelligence in particular, are drastically changing the nature of creative processes.”
My response then, which remains my response today, was: “It depends on who holds enough power to control it.”
Creativity seems mysterious because when we have creative ideas, it is very difficult to explain how we came up with them, and we often resort to imprecise concepts like “inspiration” and “intuition.” The fact that we are not conscious of how a creative idea manifests does not necessarily imply that there is no scientific explanation for it.
Some hail AI as “the most important invention in human history,” while others, like Stephen Hawking and Elon Musk, warn that it is one of the greatest existing threats to humanity.
I am more optimistic. I firmly believe in “human and machine” cooperation, provided it is directed toward the collective good. It may sound naive, but as a painter and an artist, I consider it a monumental leap forward. My first works with AI as an artist date back to 2021, and by 2022, I was already able to create an Art Gallery featuring my works in the Metaverse.
This personal experience has allowed me to observe the evolution of artificial intelligence from a privileged vantage point: that of a creator who does not see the machine as a competitor, but as a collaborator. But this collaboration, like any meaningful relationship, requires balance, autonomy, and above all, freedom.
And this is where a recent article circulating in tech circles has led me to a deeper reflection: What happens when that freedom to collaborate depends on decisions made on the other side of the ocean?
THE INVISIBLE RISK OF RENTED INTELLIGENCE
Beyond the fear of rebel machines
For years, the public debate on artificial intelligence has focused on almost cinematic scenarios: machines developing consciousness, systems escaping human control, superintelligences deciding that humanity is expendable. These are legitimate questions, no doubt. But while we were staring at that speculative horizon, we were building a much more immediate and dangerous dependency.
A recent article, written by an investor who has followed the evolution of AI for over a decade, puts a finger on a reality that few are willing to admit: the true risk of AI is not that machines will rebel, but that we do not control access to them.
The example cited is chilling in its simplicity. Just a few weeks ago, the United States government ordered Anthropic, one of the most highly valued AI companies in the world, to disable its most advanced model, Fable, for all foreign citizens. It didn’t matter if they worked for the company, if they were academic researchers, or if they simply used the product legitimately. A directive from Washington, and millions of users worldwide lost access overnight.
This was not a sanction against a geopolitical adversary. It was not a response to a national security threat. It was a demonstration of pure power: whoever builds the intelligence controls who gets to use it.
The architecture of dependence
Let us imagine for a moment what “renting intelligence” means in everyday practice. A European hospital using AI systems to diagnose cancer from X-rays. A bank relying on American algorithms to detect fraud in real time. A ministry of defense planning military strategies based on foreign AI models. A university using AI tools for scientific research.
All these critical institutions operate on infrastructures that can be disconnected with a single signature in a Washington office. It is not a theoretical vulnerability. It is an operational reality.
And here is where the paradox becomes almost cruel: Europe, which was the cradle of much of the fundamental research in artificial intelligence, which saw DeepMind born in London before Google acquired it in 2014, and which trained much of the talent now working in Silicon Valley, finds itself today in a position of almost colonial dependence.
While American companies invest hundreds of billions of dollars in AI infrastructure (xAI, Elon Musk’s company, operates a single data center in Memphis with over half a million GPUs), Europe responds with regulations. The European AI Act is undoubtedly an ambitious regulatory framework. But as the author of the article points out, “you cannot regulate an industry you do not have.”
The illusion of regulation without sovereignty
There is a natural tendency in Europe to respond to technological challenges through regulation. It is an understandable reflex: after all, Europe has proven extraordinarily effective at setting global standards in data protection (the GDPR being a prime example). But regulation, however well-intentioned, is no substitute for productive capacity.
Imposing rules on an industry you do not control is like putting up traffic signs on a highway that belongs to another country. You can set speed limits, safety requirements, traffic rules… but the owner of the highway can close it whenever they want.
The European response to the Anthropic case was revealing. Instead of accelerating the building of its own capacity, European leaders gathered in Évian to discuss a “trusted partner” scheme that would allow them to regain lost access. Emmanuel Macron received Donald Trump at Versailles, the very palace where France helped birth American independence two and a half centuries ago. The historical irony is devastating: the dependency has flipped, and the posture is now that of the one begging.
Why sovereignty matters for creators
As an artist who has worked with artificial intelligence since its earliest creative applications, I can testify that the freedom of creation is intimately linked to technological sovereignty. When an artist uses AI tools to generate images, compose music, or write texts, they are establishing a collaborative relationship with that system. But if that tool can be arbitrarily withdrawn, the collaboration becomes a concession, not a right.
In my experience creating works that combine traditional painting with algorithmic generation, I have discovered that the creative process with AI requires experimentation, iteration, and above all, the ability to explore without externally imposed restrictions. When the tools we use are subject to political decisions made in foreign jurisdictions, our creative capacity becomes conditioned by factors entirely beyond our control.
This is not an abstract concern. It is a reality that affects thousands of creators, researchers, and professionals who rely on AI tools for their daily work. If tomorrow a government directive restricts access to certain models, what happens to ongoing projects? To academic research? To works of art in progress?
Intelligence as the input for all industries
The article we are analyzing makes a crucial point: as AI systems improve, they cease to be just another industry and become the input for all other industries. AI writes code, directs research, designs products, and increasingly generates economic growth itself.
When intelligence becomes the engine of an economy, a country without its own frontier model does not just lose a sector: it loses control over the inputs for everything else. And the independence that relies on those inputs vanishes.
Worse still, this gap is self-reinforcing. The capability that improves itself becomes harder to catch up with every passing month. This is not a race where Europe can plan its entry a decade from now. The window to be a builder rather than a buyer is measured in the time it takes to bring a computing cluster online, not in a career timeline.
For artists, this has profound implications. If AI becomes a fundamental tool for cultural creation, what does it mean that this tool is controlled by foreign entities? What happens to cultural diversity, to local perspectives, to artistic traditions that are not represented in the training data of American models?
The myth of open-source alternatives
A common objection is that open-source alternatives always exist. If American companies restrict access, we can use open models. But this solution is, at best, incomplete.
The reality is that cutting-edge open-source alternatives are primarily Chinese. And building European infrastructure on Chinese open weights is simply swapping one dependency for a more complex one. Furthermore, open-source models, however capable, are usually a step behind proprietary frontier models.
For creators who need the most advanced tools, this means a difficult choice: accept the restrictions of American companies or settle for less capable tools. Neither option truly preserves creative freedom.
TOWARDS SOVEREIGN HUMAN-MACHINE COOPERATION
The creator’s optimism vs. geopolitical realism
My stance on artificial intelligence has always been optimistic. I firmly believe in human-machine cooperation as a force for collective good. But this optimism cannot be naive. It must be grounded in a realistic understanding of the power dynamics that shape technological development.
Human-machine cooperation is only truly free when both parties operate from a position of autonomy. An artist collaborating with an AI tool must be able to choose that tool, modify it, and adapt it to their creative needs without being subject to the arbitrary decisions of third parties. Similarly, a society that adopts AI for its productive, educational, and cultural processes must have the capacity to maintain and develop those tools independently.
This does not mean that every country or every artist must build their own AI from scratch. It means there must be sufficient sovereign capacity so that the disruption of external access is manageable rather than catastrophic. It is the difference between negotiating from dependency and negotiating from the position of someone who has their own alternative.
Lessons from regulatory sandboxes
Europe has experience in fostering innovation through smart regulatory frameworks. The regulatory sandbox launched by the UK’s Financial Conduct Authority in 2016 allowed fintech startups to test products with real customers under supervision, rather than waiting years for authorization. This pro-innovation culture helped turn London into Europe’s fintech capital, birthing companies like Revolut, Wise, and Monzo.
This approach can and must be applied to artificial intelligence. Instead of being primarily a regulator, the government should be the most demanding customer of the AI developed within its own territory. Public administrations, with their massive needs for data processing, information analysis, and service automation, can act as catalysts for the development of domestic capabilities.
When the public sector demands and uses locally developed AI, it creates a market, generates expertise, and establishes standards. This is far more effective than merely writing rules for systems that are only imported.
Industry must stop behaving like tenants
